input.content:
Architecture review — typeid-python

2026-06-12 · branch claude/gifted-hopper-qd8wfb · ~850 LOC

module seam leakage deep module

1 · Move suffix validity into the base32 module

Strong in-process latent bug — verified

typeid/base32.py · typeid/validation.py · typeid/cli.py

Before

flowchart TB
  FS["TypeID.from_string"] --> VS["validate_suffix
re-implements alphabet rules"]
  VS --> DEC["base32.decode
own check is broken:
'and'-chain never fires"]
  CLI["cli decode"] -- skips validation --> DEC
  classDef leak stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px;
  classDef dup fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#ca8a04;
  class CLI,DEC leak
  class VS dup

typeid decode user_…u2 → prints a garbage UUID, no error

After

flowchart TB
  FS["TypeID.from_string"] --> B32
  CLI["cli decode"] --> B32
  subgraph B32["base32 — deep"]
    direction TB
    D2["decode: rejects any invalid
character, typed error"]
  end
  style B32 fill:#0f172a,color:#f8fafc,stroke:#0f172a
  style D2 fill:#1e293b,color:#94a3b8,stroke:#334155

One copy of the alphabet rules, behind one interface. Every caller validated for free.

Problem. Suffix validity is duplicated: validate_suffix re-implements the alphabet rules, while base32.decode's own check is a mistranslated port — its 26 conditions are joined with and instead of or, so it raises only when every character is invalid. The CLI crosses the seam straight to decode and silently prints a wrong UUID for invalid input (reproduced on this branch).

Solution. Make decode own validity — reject any invalid character with a typed error — and shrink validate_suffix to the checks base32 can't know (length, leading-char ≤ 7), or delete it into base32.

  • Locality: validity bugs concentrate in base32
  • Latent CLI bug dies
  • Deletion test: validate_suffix mostly vanishes
  • Tests hit one interface, not two

2 · Bytes-native base32 interface

Worth exploring in-process

typeid/base32.py · typeid/typeid.py · typeid/cli.py · tests/test_base32.py

Before — interface nearly as complex as the implementation

interface

encode(src: list[int]) → str
decode(s: str) → list[int]
+ caller must convert:
list(uuid.bytes)
bytes(decode(…))

implementation

bit-shuffling, TABLE

list[int] is the implementation's working type, leaking through the interface. Four call sites pay the conversion tax.

After — deep

interface

encode(bytes) → str · decode(str) → bytes

implementation

bit-shuffling, TABLE,
conversions absorbed

Callers pass what they have (uuid.bytes) and get what they need (UUID(bytes=…)). No conversion at any call site.

Problem. The base32 interface speaks list[int] — an implementation detail — so all four callers wrap every call in list(…) / bytes(…) conversions.

Solution. Interface becomes bytes → str and str → bytes; conversions move inside the implementation.

  • Interface shrinks; implementation absorbs conversions
  • Leverage: one interface, four call sites simplify
  • Pairs naturally with candidate 1

3 · Make the CLI an adapter over TypeID.from_string

Worth exploring in-process

typeid/cli.py · typeid/typeid.py · typeid/__init__.py

Before — CLI re-implements parsing

flowchart TB
  CLI["cli decode"] --> GPS["get_prefix_and_suffix
exported, returns bare tuple"]
  CLI -- no prefix validation --> OUT["prints type: USER"]
  CLI --> DEC["base32.decode"]
  FS["TypeID.from_string"] --> GPS
  FS --> VAL["validate_prefix / suffix"]
  classDef leak stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px;
  class CLI,OUT leak

After — one parsing interface

flowchart TB
  CLI["cli decode — adapter"] --> TID
  LIB["library callers"] --> TID
  subgraph TID["TypeID.from_string — deep"]
    direction TB
    INT["parse · validate · decode
get_prefix_and_suffix internal"]
  end
  style TID fill:#0f172a,color:#f8fafc,stroke:#0f172a
  style INT fill:#1e293b,color:#94a3b8,stroke:#334155

Problem. The CLI's decode command rebuilds TypeID.from_string by hand — splitting, decoding, skipping prefix and suffix validation entirely — so typeid decode USER_… happily prints an invalid TypeID's parts. get_prefix_and_suffix sits in __all__ as a second, weaker parsing interface returning a bare tuple.

Solution. CLI becomes an adapter: TypeID.from_string(encoded) then .prefix / .uuid; get_prefix_and_suffix is demoted from interface to implementation.

  • Locality: parsing rules live in one module
  • CLI validation gap closes
  • Public interface loses one weak entry point
  • CLI tests become spec tests

4 · Retire the deprecated construction functions

Speculative in-process breaking — needs a major bump

typeid/typeid.py · typeid/__init__.py · typeid/cli.py

Before — two names per construction path

TypeID.from_string
from_string deprecated since 2023
TypeID.from_uuid
from_uuid deprecated — yet the CLI still calls it

After

TypeID.from_string
TypeID.from_uuid
Interface halves. DeprecationWarnings the CLI was silently emitting disappear with it.

Problem. Module-level from_string / from_uuid are pass-throughs that fail the deletion test — deleting them moves nothing, it only removes a second name for the same behaviour. The library's own CLI still calls the deprecated one.

Solution. Point the CLI at the classmethods now; drop the module-level pair from __all__ at the next major version.

  • Interface shrinks: one construction path
  • Deletion test: pure pass-throughs

Top recommendation

Move suffix validity into the base32 module

It's the one candidate where the missing depth is already costing correctness: the duplicated validity rules let a broken check ship inside decode, and the CLI crosses that seam unprotected — typeid decode prints garbage UUIDs for invalid input today. Deepening base32 fixes the bug, deletes the duplication, and sets up candidates 2 and 3 to follow cheaply.

  classDef leak stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px;
  class CLI,OUT leak

After — one parsing interface

flowchart TB
  CLI["cli decode — adapter"] --> TID
  LIB["library callers"] --> TID
  subgraph TID["TypeID.from_string — deep"]
    direction TB
    INT["parse · validate · decode
get_prefix_and_suffix internal"]
  end
  style TID fill:#0f172a,color:#f8fafc,stroke:#0f172a
  style INT fill:#1e293b,color:#94a3b8,stroke:#334155

Problem. The CLI's decode command rebuilds TypeID.from_string by hand — splitting, decoding, skipping prefix and suffix validation entirely — so typeid decode USER_… happily prints an invalid TypeID's parts. get_prefix_and_suffix sits in __all__ as a second, weaker parsing interface returning a bare tuple.

Solution. CLI becomes an adapter: TypeID.from_string(encoded) then .prefix / .uuid; get_prefix_and_suffix is demoted from interface to implementation.

  • Locality: parsing rules live in one module
  • CLI validation gap closes
  • Public interface loses one weak entry point
  • CLI tests become spec tests

4 · Retire the deprecated construction functions

Speculative in-process breaking — needs a major bump

typeid/typeid.py · typeid/__init__.py · typeid/cli.py

Before — two names per construction path

TypeID.from_string
from_string deprecated since 2023
TypeID.from_uuid
from_uuid deprecated — yet the CLI still calls it

After

TypeID.from_string
TypeID.from_uuid
Interface halves. DeprecationWarnings the CLI was silently emitting disappear with it.

Problem. Module-level from_string / from_uuid are pass-throughs that fail the deletion test — deleting them moves nothing, it only removes a second name for the same behaviour. The library's own CLI still calls the deprecated one.

Solution. Point the CLI at the classmethods now; drop the module-level pair from __all__ at the next major version.

  • Interface shrinks: one construction path
  • Deletion test: pure pass-throughs

Top recommendation

Move suffix validity into the base32 module

It's the one candidate where the missing depth is already costing correctness: the duplicated validity rules let a broken check ship inside decode, and the CLI crosses that seam unprotected — typeid decode prints garbage UUIDs for invalid input today. Deepening base32 fixes the bug, deletes the duplication, and sets up candidates 2 and 3 to follow cheaply.

toolUseResult.caption: Architecture review of typeid-python — 4 deepening candidates (open in a browser; diagrams render via CDN)

content.text:
The review is done — the report is at `/tmp/architecture-review-20260612-182500.html` (sent above; open it in a browser, Tailwind/Mermaid load from CDN). Four deepening candidates, in brief:

1. **Move suffix validity into the base32 module** — `Strong`. Validity knowledge is duplicated: `validate_suffix` re-implements the alphabet rules while `base32.decode`'s own check is a mistranslated port — 26 conditions joined with `and` instead of `or`, so it only raises when *every* character is invalid. I verified the consequence on this branch: `typeid decode user_…u2` (invalid character `u`) silently prints a garbage UUID, because the CLI crosses the seam straight to `decode`, bypassing the working copy of the rules. Deepening `decode` to own validity fixes a real bug and collapses the duplication.

2. **Bytes-native base32 interface** — `Worth exploring`. `encode(list[int])`/`decode → list[int]` leaks the implementation's working type; all four call sites pay a `list(…)`/`bytes(…)` conversion tax. Interface should be `bytes → str` / `str → bytes`.

3. **Make the CLI an adapter over `TypeID.from_string`** — `Worth exploring`. The CLI's `decode` command rebuilds parsing by hand and skips prefix *and* suffix validation (`typeid decode USER_…` accepts an invalid prefix today); `get_prefix_and_suffix` sits in `__all__` as a second, weaker parsing interface returning a bare tuple.

4. **Retire the deprecated module-level `from_string`/`from_uuid`** — `Speculative` (breaking, needs a major bump). Pure pass-throughs that fail the deletion test; amusingly, the CLI itself still calls the deprecated one.

**Top recommendation: candidate 1** — it's the only one where the missing depth is already costing correctness, and it makes 2 and 3 cheap follow-ons.

Which of these would you like to explore?

content.content: Launching skill: setup-matt-pocock-skills

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /root/.claude/skills/setup-matt-pocock-skills

# Setup Matt Pocock's Skills

Scaffold the per-repo configuration that the engineering skills assume:

- **Issue tracker** — where issues live (GitHub by default; local markdown is also supported out of the box)
- **Triage labels** — the strings used for the five canonical triage roles
- **Domain docs** — where `CONTEXT.md` and ADRs live, and the consumer rules for reading them

This is a prompt-driven skill, not a deterministic script. Explore, present what you found, confirm with the user, then write.

## Process

### 1. Explore

Look at the current repo to understand its starting state. Read whatever exists; don't assume:

- `git remote -v` and `.git/config` — is this a GitHub repo? Which one?
- `AGENTS.md` and `CLAUDE.md` at the repo root — does either exist? Is there already an `## Agent skills` section in either?
- `CONTEXT.md` and `CONTEXT-MAP.md` at the repo root
- `docs/adr/` and any `src/*/docs/adr/` directories
- `docs/agents/` — does this skill's prior output already exist?
- `.scratch/` — sign that a local-markdown issue tracker convention is already in use

### 2. Present findings and ask

Summarise what's present and what's missing. Then walk the user through the three decisions **one at a time** — present a section, get the user's answer, then move to the next. Don't dump all three at once.

Assume the user does not know what these terms mean. Each section starts with a short explainer (what it is, why these skills need it, what changes if they pick differently). Then show the choices and the default.

**Section A — Issue tracker.**

> Explainer: The "issue tracker" is where issues live for this repo. Skills like `to-issues`, `triage`, `to-prd`, and `qa` read from and write to it — they need to know whether to call `gh issue create`, write a markdown file under `.scratch/`, or follow some other workflow you describe. Pick the place you actually track work for this repo.

Default posture: these skills were designed for GitHub. If a `git remote` points at GitHub, propose that. If a `git remote` points at GitLab (`gitlab.com` or a self-hosted host), propose GitLab. Otherwise (or if the user prefers), offer:

- **GitHub** — issues live in the repo's GitHub Issues (uses the `gh` CLI)
- **GitLab** — issues live in the repo's GitLab Issues (uses the [`glab`](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/cli) CLI)
- **Local markdown** — issues live as files under `.scratch/<feature>/` in this repo (good for solo projects or repos without a remote)
- **Other** (Jira, Linear, etc.) — ask the user to describe the workflow in one paragraph; the skill will record it as freeform prose

**Section B — Triage label vocabulary.**

> Explainer: When the `triage` skill processes an incoming issue, it moves it through a state machine — needs evaluation, waiting on reporter, ready for an AFK agent to pick up, ready for a human, or won't fix. To do that, it needs to apply labels (or the equivalent in your issue tracker) that match strings *you've actually configured*. If your repo already uses different label names (e.g. `bug:triage` instead of `needs-triage`), map them here so the skill applies the right ones instead of creating duplicates.

The five canonical roles:

- `needs-triage` — maintainer needs to evaluate
- `needs-info` — waiting on reporter
- `ready-for-agent` — fully specified, AFK-ready (an agent can pick it up with no human context)
- `ready-for-human` — needs human implementation
- `wontfix` — will not be actioned

Default: each role's string equals its name. Ask the user if they want to override any. If their issue tracker has no existing labels, the defaults are fine.

**Section C — Domain docs.**

> Explainer: Some skills (`improve-codebase-architecture`, `diagnose`, `tdd`) read a `CONTEXT.md` file to learn the project's domain language, and `docs/adr/` for past architectural decisions. They need to know whether the repo has one global context or multiple (e.g. a monorepo with separate frontend/backend contexts) so they look in the right place.

Confirm the layout:

- **Single-context** — one `CONTEXT.md` + `docs/adr/` at the repo root. Most repos are this.
- **Multi-context** — `CONTEXT-MAP.md` at the root pointing to per-context `CONTEXT.md` files (typically a monorepo).

### 3. Confirm and edit

Show the user a draft of:

- The `## Agent skills` block to add to whichever of `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md` is being edited (see step 4 for selection rules)
- The contents of `docs/agents/issue-tracker.md`, `docs/agents/triage-labels.md`, `docs/agents/domain.md`

Let them edit before writing.

### 4. Write

**Pick the file to edit:**

- If `CLAUDE.md` exists, edit it.
- Else if `AGENTS.md` exists, edit it.
- If neither exists, ask the user which one to create — don't pick for them.

Never create `AGENTS.md` when `CLAUDE.md` already exists (or vice versa) — always edit the one that's already there.

If an `## Agent skills` block already exists in the chosen file, update its contents in-place rather than appending a duplicate. Don't overwrite user edits to the surrounding sections.

The block:

```markdown
## Agent skills

### Issue tracker

[one-line summary of where issues are tracked]. See `docs/agents/issue-tracker.md`.

### Triage labels

[one-line summary of the label vocabulary]. See `docs/agents/triage-labels.md`.

### Domain docs

[one-line summary of layout — "single-context" or "multi-context"]. See `docs/agents/domain.md`.
```

Then write the three docs files using the seed templates in this skill folder as a starting point:

- [issue-tracker-github.md](./issue-tracker-github.md) — GitHub issue tracker
- [issue-tracker-gitlab.md](./issue-tracker-gitlab.md) — GitLab issue tracker
- [issue-tracker-local.md](./issue-tracker-local.md) — local-markdown issue tracker
- [triage-labels.md](./triage-labels.md) — label mapping
- [domain.md](./domain.md) — domain doc consumer rules + layout

For "other" issue trackers, write `docs/agents/issue-tracker.md` from scratch using the user's description.

### 5. Done

Tell the user the setup is complete and which engineering skills will now read from these files. Mention they can edit `docs/agents/*.md` directly later — re-running this skill is only necessary if they want to switch issue trackers or restart from scratch.

input.command: git remote -v && ls -a && ls docs 2>/dev/null; ls .scratch 2>/dev/null; ls docs/agents 2>/dev/null; cat CLAUDE.md 2>/dev/null; cat AGENTS.md 2>/dev/null; cat CONTEXT.md 2>/dev/null; echo "exit: $?"

content.content:
# Issue tracker: GitHub

Issues and PRDs for this repo live as GitHub issues. Use the `gh` CLI for all operations.

## Conventions

- **Create an issue**: `gh issue create --title "..." --body "..."`. Use a heredoc for multi-line bodies.
- **Read an issue**: `gh issue view <number> --comments`, filtering comments by `jq` and also fetching labels.
- **List issues**: `gh issue list --state open --json number,title,body,labels,comments --jq '[.[] | {number, title, body, labels: [.labels[].name], comments: [.comments[].body]}]'` with appropriate `--label` and `--state` filters.
- **Comment on an issue**: `gh issue comment <number> --body "..."`
- **Apply / remove labels**: `gh issue edit <number> --add-label "..."` / `--remove-label "..."`
- **Close**: `gh issue close <number> --comment "..."`

Infer the repo from `git remote -v` — `gh` does this automatically when run inside a clone.

## When a skill says "publish to the issue tracker"

Create a GitHub issue.

## When a skill says "fetch the relevant ticket"

Run `gh issue view <number> --comments`.
# Triage Labels

The skills speak in terms of five canonical triage roles. This file maps those roles to the actual label strings used in this repo's issue tracker.

| Label in mattpocock/skills | Label in our tracker | Meaning                                  |
| -------------------------- | -------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| `needs-triage`             | `needs-triage`       | Maintainer needs to evaluate this issue  |
| `needs-info`               | `needs-info`         | Waiting on reporter for more information |
| `ready-for-agent`          | `ready-for-agent`    | Fully specified, ready for an AFK agent  |
| `ready-for-human`          | `ready-for-human`    | Requires human implementation            |
| `wontfix`                  | `wontfix`            | Will not be actioned                     |

When a skill mentions a role (e.g. "apply the AFK-ready triage label"), use the corresponding label string from this table.

Edit the right-hand column to match whatever vocabulary you actually use.
# Domain Docs

How the engineering skills should consume this repo's domain documentation when exploring the codebase.

## Before exploring, read these

- **`CONTEXT.md`** at the repo root, or
- **`CONTEXT-MAP.md`** at the repo root if it exists — it points at one `CONTEXT.md` per context. Read each one relevant to the topic.
- **`docs/adr/`** — read ADRs that touch the area you're about to work in. In multi-context repos, also check `src/<context>/docs/adr/` for context-scoped decisions.

If any of these files don't exist, **proceed silently**. Don't flag their absence; don't suggest creating them upfront. The producer skill (`/grill-with-docs`) creates them lazily when terms or decisions actually get resolved.

## File structure

Single-context repo (most repos):

```
/
├── CONTEXT.md
├── docs/adr/
│   ├── 0001-event-sourced-orders.md
│   └── 0002-postgres-for-write-model.md
└── src/
```

Multi-context repo (presence of `CONTEXT-MAP.md` at the root):

```
/
├── CONTEXT-MAP.md
├── docs/adr/                          ← system-wide decisions
└── src/
    ├── ordering/
    │   ├── CONTEXT.md
    │   └── docs/adr/                  ← context-specific decisions
    └── billing/
        ├── CONTEXT.md
        └── docs/adr/
```

## Use the glossary's vocabulary

When your output names a domain concept (in an issue title, a refactor proposal, a hypothesis, a test name), use the term as defined in `CONTEXT.md`. Don't drift to synonyms the glossary explicitly avoids.

If the concept you need isn't in the glossary yet, that's a signal — either you're inventing language the project doesn't use (reconsider) or there's a real gap (note it for `/grill-with-docs`).

## Flag ADR conflicts

If your output contradicts an existing ADR, surface it explicitly rather than silently overriding:

> _Contradicts ADR-0007 (event-sourced orders) — but worth reopening because…_

toolUseResult.content:
# Issue tracker: GitHub

Issues and PRDs for this repo live as GitHub issues on `oerd/typeid-python`. Use the `gh` CLI for all operations.

## Conventions

- **Create an issue**: `gh issue create --title "..." --body "..."`. Use a heredoc for multi-line bodies.
- **Read an issue**: `gh issue view <number> --comments`, filtering comments by `jq` and also fetching labels.
- **List issues**: `gh issue list --state open --json number,title,body,labels,comments --jq '[.[] | {number, title, body, labels: [.labels[].name], comments: [.comments[].body]}]'` with appropriate `--label` and `--state` filters.
- **Comment on an issue**: `gh issue comment <number> --body "..."`
- **Apply / remove labels**: `gh issue edit <number> --add-label "..."` / `--remove-label "..."`
- **Close**: `gh issue close <number> --comment "..."`

Infer the repo from `git remote -v` — `gh` does this automatically when run inside a clone.

In Claude Code cloud sessions, `gh` is not installed; use the built-in GitHub tools (`mcp__github__*`) for the equivalent operations instead.

## When a skill says "publish to the issue tracker"

Create a GitHub issue.

## When a skill says "fetch the relevant ticket"

Run `gh issue view <number> --comments`.

+- **Comment on an issue**: `gh issue comment <number> --body "..."`
+- **Apply / remove labels**: `gh issue edit <number> --add-label "..."` / `--remove-label "..."`
+- **Close**: `gh issue close <number> --comment "..."`

| `ready-for-human`          | `ready-for-human`    | Requires human implementation            |
| `wontfix`                  | `wontfix`            | Will not be actioned                     |

Based on the search results, here's information about the Entire CLI and its installation/enablement process:

## Entire CLI Overview

Entire CLI hooks into your Git workflow to capture AI agent sessions as you work. Sessions are indexed alongside commits, creating a searchable record of how code was written in your repo.

## Installation

You can install Entire via Homebrew with `brew tap entireio/tap` and `brew install --cask entire`, or via install.sh with `curl -fsSL https://entire.io/install.sh | bash`

## The `entire enable` Command

On a repo that has not been enabled yet, entire enable runs the initial enable flow: it creates Entire settings, installs git hooks, and prompts you to choose which agent hooks to install.

To enable a specific agent non-interactively, use entire enable --agent <n> (for example, entire enable --agent cursor).

## Checkpoints

Checkpoints are created when you or the agent make a git commit. All session metadata is stored on a separate entire/checkpoints/v1 branch.

## Supported AI Agents

Entire works with Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Pi, and more.

The tool is designed to provide a complete record of AI-assisted coding work, with checkpoints serving as "save points" you can rewind to if needed.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
Entire CLI 0.7.5
Go version: go1.26.4
OS/Arch: linux/amd64
Enable Entire with session tracking for your AI agent workflows.

If Entire is not yet configured, this runs the full configuration flow.
If Entire is already configured but disabled, this re-enables it.

If the current directory is not a git repository, Entire can initialize one
for you and (optionally) create a matching GitHub repository via the gh CLI.

Usage:
  entire enable [flags]

Flags:
      --absolute-git-hook-path          Embed full binary path in git hooks (for GUI git clients that don't source shell profiles)
      --agent string                    Agent to set up hooks for (e.g., claude-code, codex, copilot-cli, cursor, factoryai-droid, gemini, opencode, pi; external agents on $PATH are also available). Enables non-interactive mode.
      --checkpoint-remote string        Checkpoint remote in provider:owner/repo format (e.g., github:org/checkpoints-repo)
  -f, --force                           Force reinstall hooks (removes existing Entire hooks first)
  -h, --help                            help for enable
      --init-repo                       If not a git repo, initialize one non-interactively
      --initial-commit-message string   Commit message for the initial commit when bootstrapping a new repo
      --local                           Write settings to .entire/settings.local.json instead of .entire/settings.json
      --no-github                       Initialize local git repo only; skip creating a GitHub remote
      --no-init-repo                    If not a git repo, exit instead of prompting to initialize one
      --project                         Write settings to .entire/settings.json even if it already exists
      --repo-name string                GitHub repository name for the new repo (used when bootstrapping)
      --repo-owner string               GitHub user or organization login for the new repo
      --repo-visibility string          GitHub repository visibility: public, private, or internal
      --skip-initial-commit             Don't create the initial commit when bootstrapping a new repo
      --skip-push-sessions              Disable automatic pushing of session logs on git push
      --telemetry                       Enable anonymous usage analytics (default true)
  -y, --yes                             Accept all defaults without prompting (in a non-repo directory: init git, create private GitHub repo, commit; then enable all agents and accept telemetry)

content.text:
{"number":3,"title":"chore: trim ruff rules to a sensible library set","body":"## Summary

Trims the ruff configuration from a kitchen-sink rule selection (~40 rule families, including Django, pandas, and Airflow leftovers) down to a lean set that fits a small library, and protects the base32 hex lookup table from formatter churn.

### Lint rules (`pyproject.toml`)

- **Kept:** pyflakes (`F`), pycodestyle (`E`/`W`), isort (`I`), pep8-naming (`N`), pyupgrade (`UP`), bugbear (`B`), comprehensions (`C4`), bandit (`S`), simplify (`SIM`), pytest-style (`PT`), and ruff-specific (`RUF`) rules.
- **Dropped:** framework rules that don't apply (`DJ`, `PD`, `ASYNC`, `G`), pedantic style families responsible for nearly all 58 pre-existing violations (`PL` magic-value comparisons, `TRY003`/`EM` exception-message style, `FBT`, `SLF`, `PTH`, `ERA`, …), and `COM` (flake8-commas), which ruff itself warns conflicts with the formatter and is a key driver of unwanted reformatting (forced trailing commas make the formatter explode lists one element per line).
- **Cleanups:** removed dead `UP038` config (rule deleted from ruff), removed Django-style `extend-exclude` patterns, scoped `S101` to `tests/` and `examples/` instead of a global ignore, ignored `N818` (renaming the `*Exception` classes would break the public API).

### Hex table protection (`typeid/base32.py`)

The 256-entry byte-to-index table was sprawled one value per line (282 lines). It is now a 16×16 grid — one row per 16 ASCII codes — wrapped in `# fmt: off` / `# fmt: on` so `ruff format` can never restructure it. Values verified byte-for-byte identical against the original; round-trip encode/decode confirmed. Also removed the stale blanket `# noqa`. The file shrank from 360 to 96 lines.

### Code fixes surfaced by the trimmed rule set

- `stacklevel=2` on the two deprecation warnings (B028)
- removed unnecessary list comprehension inside `any()` (C419)
- untwisted a `x if not y` conditional (SIM212)
- `__repr__` modernized from `%`-formatting to an f-string (UP031)
- applied the one outstanding formatter normalization in `errors.py`

## Verification

- `ruff check`: passes clean
- `ruff format`: idempotent (re-running changes nothing)
- `pytest`: 28/28 passing

https://claude.ai/code/session_01KnXyVwZRNZ2Q2eaxAsfJaB

input.body:
## Summary

Activates [Entire](https://github.com/entireio/cli) session tracking on this repository:

- `.entire/settings.json` — project-level Entire config (`enabled: true`)
- `.claude/settings.json` — Claude Code hook configuration so agent sessions are captured (all hooks no-op gracefully when the `entire` binary isn't on PATH)
- `.claude/agents/entire-search.md` — the Entire search subagent
- `.entire/.gitignore` — keeps local Entire state out of git

Git hooks (`post-commit`, `pre-push`, etc.) and the `entire/checkpoints/v1` metadata branch are managed locally by the CLI and are not part of this diff.

Note: the repo uses pre-commit; if `pre-commit install` reinstalls git hooks, run `entire enable` again to restore Entire's hooks.

https://claude.ai/code/session_01KnXyVwZRNZ2Q2eaxAsfJaB
input.head: claude/gifted-hopper-qd8wfb
input.title: chore: enable Entire session tracking

content.text: {"id":"3856785511","url":"https://github.com/oerd/typeid-python/pull/5"}

Done — Entire is installed and active on the repo, and the config is pushed as draft **PR #5**: https://github.com/oerd/typeid-python/pull/5

What happened:

- **Install**: the official installer (`entire.io/install.sh`) is blocked by this environment's network policy, so I downloaded the v0.7.5 release binaries from GitHub instead, verified the SHA-256 checksum against the published `checksums.txt`, and installed `entire` + `git-remote-entire` to `/usr/local/bin`.
- **Activation**: `entire enable --agent claude-code` installed 5 git hooks, wrote project config (`.entire/settings.json`), Claude Code hooks (`.claude/settings.json` — all of them no-op gracefully if `entire` isn't on PATH), the `entire-search` subagent, and created the orphan `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch for session metadata. `entire status` confirms: **Enabled · manual-commit · Claude Code**.
- **One caveat it flagged**: this repo uses pre-commit, and if `pre-commit install` reinstalls git hooks it will clobber Entire's — rerun `entire enable` after that.
- Also discovered along the way: you merged **PR #3** (the ruff work, including the AGENTS.md commit) into `main` — congrats — and the branch deletion is why this push recreated it. PR #5 therefore contains only the Entire commit.

Two notes: the binary install is local to this ephemeral container — to have `entire` in future cloud sessions, add the download/install lines to your environment's setup script (happy to draft that). And the architecture review is still waiting on your pick of candidates 1–4.

Sources: [entireio/cli on GitHub](https://github.com/entireio/cli), [Entire docs — Quickstart](https://docs.entire.io/quickstart), [entire.io](https://entire.io/)

Draft the lines that need to be added to the environment setup script.

content.text:
Here are the lines to add to your environment's setup script (claude.ai/code → your environment → **Setup script**):

```bash
# --- Entire CLI ---
# entire.io is blocked by the network policy, so install from GitHub releases.
# Pinned + checksum-verified; bump ENTIRE_VERSION to upgrade.
ENTIRE_VERSION=v0.7.5
ENTIRE_ARCH=$(uname -m | sed 's/x86_64/amd64/; s/aarch64/arm64/')
(
  cd /tmp
  curl -fsSLO "https://github.com/entireio/cli/releases/download/${ENTIRE_VERSION}/entire_linux_${ENTIRE_ARCH}.tar.gz"
  curl -fsSLO "https://github.com/entireio/cli/releases/download/${ENTIRE_VERSION}/checksums.txt"
  grep "entire_linux_${ENTIRE_ARCH}.tar.gz" checksums.txt | sha256sum -c -
  tar -xzf "entire_linux_${ENTIRE_ARCH}.tar.gz" entire git-remote-entire
  install -m 0755 entire git-remote-entire /usr/local/bin/
)

# Fresh clones have no git hooks, so re-arm Entire's (.entire/settings.json is
# already committed; --agent makes this non-interactive). Non-fatal on failure
# because the Claude Code hooks no-op when the binary is missing anyway.
entire enable --agent claude-code --force || true
```

Notes on the choices:

- **Pinned version with checksum verification** rather than `releases/latest/download/...` — the checksum line fails the script (and the session start) if the download is corrupted or tampered with, and `checksums.txt` only matches a pinned version anyway. Bump `ENTIRE_VERSION` when you want a newer release.
- **`entire enable` must re-run every session**: the `.entire/settings.json` and `.claude/settings.json` from PR #5 come with the clone, but git hooks live in `.git/hooks`, which a fresh clone doesn't have. `--force` makes the re-install idempotent, and `|| true` keeps a hiccup there from blocking session start (the docs recommend non-critical steps not be fatal).
- The subshell around the download keeps the script's working directory at the repo root, which `entire enable` needs.

And since you'll be in there anyway — the optional line to make the mattpocock skills persist across sessions too:

```bash
# --- mattpocock/skills (user-level, stays out of the repo) ---
npx -y skills add https://github.com/mattpocock/skills -g --skill '*' --agent claude-code -y || true
```

content.text:
Good news — the checkpoints branch already synced to GitHub, so the full durable picture works. "Durable" for Entire splits into four layers, each with a different home:

**1. Repo config → git (merge PR #5).** `.entire/settings.json`, the Claude Code hooks in `.claude/settings.json`, and the search subagent are ordinary committed files. Once PR #5 lands on `main`, every future clone — cloud session, your laptop, a colleague's — arrives with Entire declared. This is the part that's already done modulo the merge.

**2. The binary → environment setup script.** Containers are ephemeral, so the binary can't persist by itself — but the setup script's result is cached by the environment, so the download/install I drafted effectively bakes into the cached image rather than re-running every session. On your own machine it's even simpler: `brew tap entireio/tap && brew install --cask entire`, once.

**3. Git hooks → re-armed per clone, by design.** Hooks live in `.git/hooks`, which git never commits — this is true of every hook-based tool (pre-commit has the same property). There is no way to make them durable in the repo; the durable solution is making the *re-arming* automatic, which is exactly the `entire enable --agent claude-code --force` line in the setup script. On your laptop you run it once per clone and it sticks, since the clone persists.

**4. Session data → the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch on GitHub.** This is the one that matters most: everything Entire captures is stored as commits on that orphan branch, and its pre-push hook pushes it alongside your normal pushes. I just verified it's already on origin (`8e8c17d`), so sessions recorded in this ephemeral container outlive it — any machine that clones the repo and runs `entire` can read the full history back.

So the complete durable setup is: **merge PR #5 + paste the setup-script lines** — config rides in git, the binary rides in the cached environment, hooks re-arm themselves, and session data rides the checkpoints branch.

One genuinely optional layer beyond that: the entire.io web platform (sessions browsable outside git). That syncs over the network rather than through git, and `entire.io` is blocked by this environment's Trusted network policy — if you want cloud sessions syncing there, add `entire.io` to the environment's allowed domains. Everything in layers 1–4 works without it.

content.text: This question maps directly onto a skill I have for exactly this — SessionStart hooks for Claude Code on the web. Let me load it so my answer is grounded:

content.content: Launching skill: session-start-hook

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /root/.claude/skills/session-start-hook

# Startup Hook Skill for Claude Code on the web

Create SessionStart hooks that install dependencies so tests and linters work in Claude Code on the web sessions.

## Hook Basics

### Input (via stdin)
```json
{
  "session_id": "abc123",
  "source": "startup|resume|clear|compact",
  "transcript_path": "/path/to/transcript.jsonl",
  "permission_mode": "default",
  "hook_event_name": "SessionStart",
  "cwd": "/workspace/repo"
}
```

### Async Mode
```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

echo '{"async": true, "asyncTimeout": 300000}'

npm install
```

The hook runs in background while the session starts. Using async mode reduces latency, but introduces a race condition where the agent loop might depend on something that is being done in the startup hook before it completed.

### Environment Variables

Available environment variables:
- `$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` - Repository root path
- `$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` - Path to write environment variables
- `$CLAUDE_CODE_REMOTE` - If running in a remote environment (i.e. Claude code on the web)

Use `$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` to persist variables for the session:
```bash
echo 'export PYTHONPATH="."' >> "$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE"
```

Use `$CLAUDE_CODE_REMOTE` to only run a script in a remote env:
```bash
if [ "${CLAUDE_CODE_REMOTE:-}" != "true" ]; then
  exit 0
fi
```

## Workflow

Make a todo list for all the tasks in this workflow and work on them one after another

### 1. Analyze Dependencies

Find dependency manifests and analyze them. Examples:
- `package.json` / `package-lock.json` → npm
- `pyproject.toml` / `requirements.txt` → pip/Poetry
- `Cargo.toml` → cargo
- `go.mod` → go
- `Gemfile` → bundler

Additionally, read though any documentation (i.e. README.md or similar) to see if you can get additional context on how the environment setup works

### 2. Design Hook

Create a script that installs dependencies.

**Key principles:**
- Don't use async mode in the first iteration. Only switch to it if the user asks for it
- Write the hook only for the web unless user asks otherwise (see $CLAUDE_CODE_REMOTE)
- The container state gets cached after the hook completes, prefer dependency install methods that take advantage of that (i.e. prefer npm install over npm ci)
- Be idempotent (safe to run multiple times)
- Non-interactive (no user input)

### 3. Create Hook File

```bash
mkdir -p .claude/hooks
cat > .claude/hooks/session-start.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

echo '{"async": true, "asyncTimeout": 300000}'
# Install dependencies here
EOF

chmod +x .claude/hooks/session-start.sh
```

### 4. Register in Settings

Add to `.claude/settings.json` (create if doesn't exist):
```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "SessionStart": [
      {
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/hooks/session-start.sh"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

If `.claude/settings.json` exists, merge the hooks configuration.

### 5. Validate Hook

Run the hook script directly:

```bash
CLAUDE_CODE_REMOTE=true ./.claude/hooks/session-start.sh
```

IMPORTANT: Verify dependencies are installed and script completes successfully.

### 6. Validate Linter

IMPORTANT: Figure out what the right command is to run the linters and run it for an example file. No need to lint the whole project. If there are any issues, update the startup script accordingly and re-test.

### 7. Validate Test

IMPORTANT: Figure out what the right command is to run the tests and run it for one test. No need to run the whole test suite. If there are any issues, update the startup script accordingly and re-test.

### 8. Commit and push

Make a commit and push it to the remote branch

## Wrap up

We're all done. In your last message to the user, Provide a detailed summary to the user with the format below:

* Summary of the changes made
* Validation results
  1. ✅/‼️ Session hook execution (include details if it failed)
  2. ✅/‼️ linter execution (include details if it failed)
  3. ✅/‼️ test execution (include details if it failed)
* Hook execution mode: Syncronous
  * inform user that hook is running syncronous and the below trade-offs. Let them know that we can change it to async if they prefer faster session startup.
    * Pros: Guarantees dependencies are installed before your session starts, preventing race conditions where Claude might try to run tests or linters before they're ready
    * Cons: Your remote session will only start once the session start hook is completed
* inform user that once they merge the session start hook into their repo's default branch, all future sessions will use it.

input.content:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Only needed in Claude Code on the web; local machines manage their own setup.
if [ "${CLAUDE_CODE_REMOTE:-}" != "true" ]; then
  exit 0
fi

cd "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR"

# --- Python deps (container state is cached after the hook completes) ---
uv sync --all-extras --dev --group=build

# --- Entire CLI ---
# entire.io is blocked by the default network policy, so install the pinned,
# checksum-verified release from GitHub. Skipped when already present (cache).
ENTIRE_VERSION=v0.7.5
if ! command -v entire >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  arch=$(uname -m | sed 's/x86_64/amd64/; s/aarch64/arm64/')
  tmp=$(mktemp -d)
  (
    cd "$tmp"
    curl -fsSLO "https://github.com/entireio/cli/releases/download/${ENTIRE_VERSION}/entire_linux_${arch}.tar.gz"
    curl -fsSLO "https://github.com/entireio/cli/releases/download/${ENTIRE_VERSION}/checksums.txt"
    grep "entire_linux_${arch}.tar.gz" checksums.txt | sha256sum -c -
    tar -xzf "entire_linux_${arch}.tar.gz" entire git-remote-entire
    install -m 0755 entire git-remote-entire /usr/local/bin/
  )
  rm -rf "$tmp"
fi

# Fresh clones have no git hooks, so re-arm Entire's every session. Non-fatal:
# the Claude Code hooks in .claude/settings.json no-op when this fails.
entire enable --agent claude-code --force || true
